prompt: For most individual taxpayers, which form is the primary U.S. individual income tax return?
explanation: Individuals generally file Form 1040 (with schedules as needed).

prompt: Qualified dividends and long-term capital gains are often taxed at:

options.text: Preferential capital gains rates (0%/15%/20% tiers, subject to rules)

explanation: LTCG/qualified dividends use preferential rate schedules when requirements are met.

prompt: The standard deduction:

options.text: Is claimed instead of itemizing Schedule A deductions when it's larger/beneficial
Is the same as the personal exemption (pre-TCJA reinstatement myth)
Can never be used by married couples

explanation: Taxpayers take the greater of standard deduction or itemized deductions.

distractorExplanations.b: Personal exemptions were suspended under TCJA for years.

prompt: A taxpayer's filing status is generally determined by:

options.text: Marital status and household situation as of year-end (with specific rules)
Their state only, never federal rules

explanation: Filing status follows Code rules on marriage/dependents/household as of Dec 31 (with nuances).

prompt: An above-the-line deduction (adjustment to income) example is often:

options.text: Charitable gifts only via Schedule A always
Deductible IRA contribution or student loan interest (when eligible)
State income tax only on Schedule A
Home mortgage interest only on Schedule A

explanation: Certain adjustments reduce AGI before itemizing/standard deduction.

distractorExplanations.a: Charity is typically itemized (with limited above-the-line exceptions in some years).

prompt: The Child Tax Credit is primarily for:

options.text: Qualifying children meeting age/relationship/support/residency tests (subject to phaseouts)
Only nonresident aliens with no SSN ever

explanation: CTC has specific qualifying child and identification requirements.

prompt: Self-employment tax generally covers:

options.text: Social Security and Medicare taxes on net earnings from self-employment

explanation: SE tax approximates employee+employer FICA on SE income.

prompt: Basis of purchased stock is generally:

options.text: Cost (including commissions) adjusted for certain events

explanation: Cost basis starts with purchase price plus acquisition costs, then adjusts.

distractorExplanations.a: Mark-to-market not default for most investors.

prompt: A capital loss on stocks for individuals can generally:

options.text: Offset capital gains and then up to $3,000 of ordinary income per year ($1,500 MFS), with carryforward
Offset unlimited ordinary income in the year realized
Only be used against municipal bond interest

explanation: Net capital loss deduction against ordinary income is limited annually; excess carries forward.

prompt: The kiddie tax rules can apply to:

options.text: A child's unearned income above thresholds, taxed at parents' rates/under special rules
Only wages from a paper route

explanation: Kiddie tax targets unearned income of certain children.

prompt: An eligible educator can deduct certain unreimbursed classroom expenses:

options.text: As an above-the-line educator expense deduction up to the annual limit
Only as a business bad debt
Never, after TCJA for all teachers

explanation: Educators get a limited above-the-line deduction for qualified expenses.

distractorExplanations.c: The educator expense deduction remains (itemized misc. 2% were limited).

prompt: Taxable Social Security benefits depend on:

options.text: Only the taxpayer's age at retirement
Whether they filed Schedule C ever

explanation: Up to 85% of SS may be taxable based on combined income formulas.

prompt: A traditional IRA distribution before age 59½ is often subject to:

options.text: Ordinary income tax plus 10% additional tax unless an exception applies
Self-employment tax on the full distribution

explanation: Early distributions are taxable and may face the 10% additional tax with listed exceptions.

distractorExplanations.d: Not SE tax on IRA distributions.

prompt: The American Opportunity Credit is generally available for:

options.text: Qualified education expenses for an eligible student in the first four years of postsecondary education (subject to limits/phaseouts)

explanation: AOTC targets undergraduate years with specific expense and phaseout rules.

distractorExplanations.b: Lifetime Learning is more flexible for grad; AOTC is undergrad-focused.

prompt: Estimated tax payments for individuals are generally due:

options.text: Only on April 15 for the entire year always
In quarterly installments (Apr/Jun/Sep/Jan dates, with nuances)

explanation: Pay-as-you-go via withholding/estimates; quarterly due dates apply.

distractorExplanations.d: Extension is for filing, not payment.

prompt: A partnership generally files:

options.text: Form 1065 and issues Schedule K-1s to partners

explanation: Partnerships are pass-through entities filing 1065 with K-1s.

prompt: An S corporation's income generally:

options.text: Is taxed at the entity at progressive corporate rates only
Passes through to shareholders (with basis/AAA and employment tax nuances for wages)
Is exempt from all tax forever

explanation: S corps are pass-throughs; reasonable compensation rules matter for shareholder-employees.

prompt: A C corporation files:
explanation: C corps are separate taxable entities on Form 1120.

prompt: Section 179 expensing allows eligible taxpayers to:

options.text: Expense the cost of qualifying property up to annual limits instead of depreciating over time

explanation: §179 permits immediate expensing of qualifying asset costs within limits/taxable income limits.

prompt: Qualified Business Income (QBI) deduction under §199A generally:

options.text: Applies to specified service trades/businesses without limit for all incomes
May allow up to 20% deduction of QBI for eligible pass-throughs, subject to thresholds/limitations

explanation: 199A provides a potential 20% QBI deduction with wage/UBIA and SSTB limitations.

prompt: Depreciation of business equipment typically uses:

options.text: Only fair market value each year as a deduction

explanation: MACRS is the primary federal depreciation system for most tangible property.

prompt: A sole proprietor reports business profit/loss on:
explanation: Sole props use Schedule C; SE tax via Schedule SE.

prompt: Meals for business are generally:

options.text: Subject to 50% limitation in many cases (with exceptions)

explanation: Business meals are often 50% deductible when ordinary/necessary and substantiated; rules have varied.

prompt: An LLC with one individual owner, by default for federal tax, is typically:

options.text: A disregarded entity taxed as a sole proprietorship (unless corporate election)

explanation: Single-member LLCs are disregarded by default; Form 8832/2553 elections can change classification.

distractorExplanations.b: Only if elected/default corporate in rare cases.

prompt: Payroll tax deposits for employers are generally:

options.text: Optional if the business is profitable
Required on IRS schedules (monthly/semiweekly, etc.) based on lookback
Paid only with the annual Form 1040
The same as estimated taxes for investors only

explanation: Employment tax deposits follow deposit schedules; Forms 941/940 report.

distractorExplanations.c: Employment taxes have their own returns.

prompt: At-risk rules and passive activity loss rules can:

options.text: Convert C corps to S corps automatically

explanation: §465/§469 can defer loss utilization until at-risk/passive tests are met.

prompt: A corporation's charitable contribution deduction is generally limited to:

options.text: A percentage of taxable income (commonly 10% before special rules/elections)
Zero if it has NOLs ever

explanation: Corporate charity deductions face taxable income percentage limits (with carryovers).

distractorExplanations.d: NOLs interact but don't auto-zero charity.

prompt: Cost of goods sold is most relevant for:

options.text: Businesses with inventory (gross receipts minus COGS = gross profit)
W-2 employees with no side activity

explanation: Inventory businesses compute COGS under accounting/tax inventory methods.

prompt: Reasonable compensation for S corporation shareholder-employees is important because:

options.text: Wages are subject to employment taxes while distributions generally are not
Distributions are always subject to SE tax
It increases the corporate AMT credit only

explanation: IRS expects reasonable wages before profit distributions to avoid employment tax avoidance.

distractorExplanations.c: They can/should pay wages when working.

prompt: Start-up and organizational costs are generally:

options.text: Fully deductible in year one without limit always
Subject to capitalization with limited immediate expensing and amortization rules
Treated as capital losses on Schedule D only

explanation: §195/§248 provide limited expensing plus amortization of the rest.

distractorExplanations.d: Not Schedule D capital loss treatment.

prompt: An Enrolled Agent is authorized to practice before the IRS by:

options.text: IRS enrollment (Special Enrollment Exam or qualifying IRS experience) and Circular 230 status

explanation: EAs are federally authorized tax practitioners under Circular 230.

distractorExplanations.c: CPAs also practice; EA is its own credential.

prompt: Form 2848 is used to:

options.text: Appoint a power of attorney to represent a taxpayer before the IRS

explanation: 2848 authorizes representation for specified tax matters/periods.

prompt: Form 8821 authorizes:

options.text: Tax information authorization (inspection/receipt of information) without full representation powers

explanation: 8821 is TIA—information access, not the same as POA representation.

prompt: In an IRS examination, a representative should generally:

options.text: Respond timely, provide relevant records, and advocate within Circular 230 ethics
Coach the client to fabricate receipts

explanation: Competent, ethical representation includes timely responses and honest submissions.

prompt: A collection due process (CDP) hearing request is typically filed on:

options.text: Form 12153 (within the stated deadline after NFTL/levy notice)

explanation: CDP rights are exercised via Form 12153 within the 30-day period generally.

prompt: An Offer in Compromise (OIC) is a request to:

options.text: Change filing status after the fact without criteria
Settle a tax debt for less than the full amount based on doubt as to collectibility/liability or effective tax administration

explanation: OIC is a collection alternative under specific IRS standards.

distractorExplanations.c: Penalty relief is separate (FTA, reasonable cause).

prompt: Innocent spouse relief is claimed on:
explanation: Form 8857 requests innocent spouse / separation of liability / equitable relief.

prompt: A statutory notice of deficiency (90-day letter) generally allows the taxpayer to:

options.text: Petition the U.S. Tax Court within 90 days (150 if addressed outside the U.S.)
Only pay immediately with no rights
File bankruptcy as the sole remedy

explanation: The SNOD opens Tax Court jurisdiction if petitioned timely.

prompt: Power of attorney authority ends when:

options.text: It is revoked, withdrawn, superseded, or the matter/period concludes as specified

explanation: POAs can be revoked/withdrawn and are limited to specified matters/periods.

prompt: CAF (Centralized Authorization File) numbers identify:
explanation: CAF tracks POA/TIA authorizations for practitioners.

prompt: During Appeals, the goal is typically to:

options.text: Litigate immediately in district court only
Resolve disputes fairly considering hazards of litigation without new enforcement by Appeals

explanation: IRS Independent Office of Appeals seeks case resolution considering litigation hazards.

prompt: A taxpayer interview with the IRS when represented:

options.text: May proceed with the authorized representative present/handling communications per the authorization

explanation: Authorized representatives may handle IRS contacts within the POA scope.

prompt: Penalty abatement for first-time abate (FTA) generally requires:

options.text: Meeting IRS criteria (compliance history, specific penalties, etc.)

explanation: FTA is an administrative waiver with eligibility conditions.

prompt: A closing agreement (Form 866/906 contexts) is used to:

options.text: Finally determine tax liability for specific matters when appropriate

explanation: Closing agreements finally resolve specified tax matters when executed.

prompt: Third-party authorization via checkbox on the return (designee):

options.text: Gives full POA for all years automatically
Allows IRS to discuss return processing issues with a designated person for a limited purpose/period
Replaces Form 2848 for audits of all prior years

explanation: The third-party designee is narrow (processing), not a general POA.

prompt: Circular 230 governs:

options.text: Practice before the IRS by attorneys, CPAs, EAs, and others eligible to practice

explanation: Circular 230 is the Treasury regulation for IRS practice standards.

prompt: A PTIN is required for:

options.text: Anyone who prepares or assists in preparing all or substantially all of a federal tax return for compensation
Only taxpayers filing their own returns

explanation: Paid preparers generally must have a PTIN.

prompt: Under Circular 230, a practitioner who knows of a client's omission on a prior return should:

options.text: Advise the client promptly of the omission and its consequences

explanation: Duty to inform the client; cannot amend without authorization.

prompt: Contingent fees for preparing an original return are generally:

options.text: Broadly permitted in all cases under Circular 230
Restricted; contingent fees are limited to specific circumstances (e.g., certain IRS examinations/challenges)
The same as a refund anticipation loan

explanation: Circular 230 limits contingent fees for return preparation.

prompt: Due diligence for EITC/CTC/AOTC (paid preparers) includes:

options.text: Completing Form 8867 and applying knowledge/inquiry standards
Skipping questions if the refund is large

explanation: Paid preparer due diligence rules require Form 8867 and specific checklists/inquiries.

prompt: Client confidentiality for tax practitioners means:

options.text: Never disclose client tax information except as authorized or legally required
Posting returns on social media for marketing
Selling client lists with SSNs freely
Discussing client details at a conference without consent

explanation: IRC §7216/privacy rules and ethics restrict disclosure of tax return information.

prompt: A practitioner's written tax advice should:

options.text: Be based on reasonable factual/legal assumptions and not be misleading

explanation: Circular 230 competence/diligence standards apply to advice.

prompt: Records submitted to the IRS should be:

options.text: Accurate copies/originals as appropriate; do not submit known false documents

explanation: Submitting false documents is criminal/ethical violation.

prompt: Advertising as an Enrolled Agent must not:

options.text: Claim IRS employee status or guaranteed results deceptively

explanation: Practitioners cannot falsely imply government employment or guarantee outcomes.

prompt: Conflict of interest under Circular 230 generally requires:

options.text: Ignoring the conflict if fees are high
Informed consent in writing when representation of conflicting interests is otherwise permitted, or declining representation

explanation: Conflicts need waiver/consent meeting Circular 230 conditions or withdrawal.

distractorExplanations.d: Unrelated/illegal in tax prep contexts often.

prompt: Electronic filing fraud schemes should be reported/prevented by preparers through:

options.text: Strong ID verification, secure systems, and not participating in refund fraud
Allowing clients to use the preparer's credentials

explanation: Security and due diligence combat stolen-identity refund fraud.

prompt: A tax preparer penalty under §6694 can apply for:

options.text: Understatements due to unreasonable positions / willful/reckless conduct
Filing on time with correct positions

explanation: §6694 penalizes preparers for unreasonable positions and reckless/willful understatements.

prompt: Best practice for engagement letters includes:

options.text: Defining scope, responsibilities, deadlines, and fee arrangements in writing
Authorizing the preparer to sign for the taxpayer without Form 8879/alternative e-file auth

explanation: Engagement letters reduce misunderstandings and document scope.

prompt: Return of client records upon request:

options.text: Practitioners must return client-provided records; may retain copies of their work product subject to rules/fee disputes nuances
May keep original client records permanently as ransom

explanation: Circular 230 addresses return of client records; holding originals for unpaid fees is restricted.

prompt: Competence under Circular 230 means a practitioner should:

options.text: Take only matters they can handle competently or become competent through study/consultation
Accept any matter regardless of knowledge
Rely solely on the client's legal conclusions without evaluation

explanation: Competence requires knowledge/skill or acquiring it appropriately.
